author: @veganismyname
text: that's why i am vegan. The diet is healthier, too.. Just supplementing B12, taurine, some other amino acids, minerals here and there, algae oils with DHA+EPA (instead of the fish), astaxanthin (antioxidant that helps preserve the algae oils and also has health benefits), some other vitamins, tofu (contains all essential amino acids), and lots of nuts, seeds, beans. incidentally i have better nutrition than ever before since I am actively optimizing my sources.

author: @ChadDidNothingWrong
text:
The issue is are kids being forced to work... not are kids simply working.
Working as a kid is the only reason why I enjoyed working menial jobs as an adult, while my spoiled peers were miserable and, ironically as a result never had the work ethic to move past those jobs. (And they're still working them today).

I honestly thank God I was able to work some in my pre-teen years. My peers in the States who had their parents pay for their schooling, unsurprisingly never seem to understand this-which is fine, but it's unfortunate when opinions are felt, they are in such black and white terms. Life can be simple, but not in every aspect.

author: @superdau
text:
The "40% Cobalt" number is definitely wrong. 40% of worldwide cobalt production does go into batteries. I wouldn't be surprised if after the quoting of quoting of quoting it somehow ended up being "40% of a battery". You could also think (although I don't subscribe to this idea) that some want Li ion batteries to look bad (I don't mean you!) and spread these numbers. 
Cobalt is 60% by weight of *active* material of the cathode of the LiCoO2 chemistry. The active material is a not negligible but still small portion of the cell's overall weight. Ignoring the case/pouch a lot of the cell is the electrode base material (copper/aluminium strips), the separators and the electrolyte. This battery type is mostly used in phones, laptops or other consumer gadgets and not in cars.
It is surpassed in specific energy by LiNiCoAlO2 (which Tesla uses in their batteries) with 9% cobalt in the active material and matched by LiNiMnCoO2. In total there's about 5-10 kg of cobalt in an EV battery (disclaimer I'm quoting, but have seen several numbers in that order, some based on g/kWh, some just the total itself).
Btw. there are almost no cobalt mines. More than 90% of cobalt production is a byproduct of copper and nickel mining (even in the DRC). Therefore it will be hard to increase production and battery manufacturers will have to try to come up with less cobalt dependent chemistries. 
Another issue none really talks about is graphite, the anode material. There's usually more of it in a battery than cathode material and it is used pretty much in all Li ion battery chemistries. The way it is mined in China is far from environmentally friendly.

All that said I'm fully in support of any legislation that forces manufacturers (all of them, not just battery manufacturers) to check where their raw materials (or parts, or even whole products for imports) are coming from and that they are produced to certain humane and environmental standards.

author: @hadfull9889
text: The issue of goods production for western markets using slave labor is a major issue. The shiny bauble of cheep products is an incentive to look the other way. Walking into a major retail chain selling cheep clothing for example, My nostrils are confronted by the stench of sweatshops. The stench of sweatshop is an odor which cannot be easily washed away - I have made the decision not to purchase items made by slaves. If we all took this position and accepted that goods produced in fair work places do cost more the matter would be resolved over night. Labeling laws which identify fair work products would assist in this decision making process.

It would certainly be a good idea to move away from cobalt.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think we as individuals don't really interfere - it's our government that do it on our behalf. The reason, I think, that the result of that interference is so often different from what it should be is the declared aim is different from the actual aim. For example our government tells us they are sending famine relief - yet behind that they are trying to secure some kind of trade deal - or some such double aim is going on.  Look at the aid sent to Yemen by the British government while at the same time the British are making massive arms sales are made to the Saudis who are anti houti. I don't think we as individuals can make a direct difference on foreign policy of governments or companies - but we can affect by our choices.

replies.author: @TonyGrant.
replies.text:
Thanks for your reply and thanks for all that info too. I have a pretty good working knowledge of graphene at this point thanks, in great part, to you and your passion. I think you are very generous and I thank you for that. 

I have done some unrelated experiments in the past and have some follow up projects on the back burner, which will take up a fair bit of time as it is. I could play around with some sheets but I'm not sure if I would be able to start grapheme from scratch (as much as I’d like to) – one can of worms too far. 

One in particular I want to do (apart from making a battery) is based on a Tesla turbine but with profiled discs. I want to make very thin discs with a graphene resin similar to what you used for your graphene armoured tile. 

After seeing you make graphene and seeing your processes and your transparency I trust that you are producing a good product. So I must ask - Will you be selling graphene in any form?

replies.author: @aga5897
replies.text:
Not sophistry at all. I personally have traveled and seen conditions in Africa and India.
Yes, people adapt, hence child labour in Cobalt mines.
No, Foreign intervention does not help in any way whatsoever.
When the market for Cobalt disappears if/when you make a ground-breaking battery, it will not Benefit All.
Those kids and their families will have to adapt or die.
The facts are out there - just go visit and see, and it is not so expensive to make it prohibitive.

Edit:

I would Certainly donate to see a Robert Murray Smith video from Africa !
Anyone else up for the ride ?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think we as individuals don't really interfere - it's our government that do it on our behalf. The reason, I think, that the result of that interference is so often different from what it should be is the declared aim is different from the actual aim. For example our government tells us they are sending famine relief - yet behind that they are trying to secure some kind of trade deal - or some such double aim is going on.  Look at the aid sent to Yemen by the British government while at the same time the British are making massive arms sales are made to the Saudis who are anti houti. I don't think we as individuals can make a direct difference on foreign policy of governments or companies - but we can affect by our choices.

author: @chrissweet2309
text:
Great points you have made. It is funny that you mentioned these things that are all true.
Until I became a whole raw food eater, never did I think about the sourcing of the food, I only cared that I could get a big mac anytime I wanted and there it was. We as a culture only generally think about change through like voting at the poles for the next political leader, the reality is we are always voting for what we want and how we want it. For instance, when we purchase food, it is at the register that we are casting our vote, if we are buying boxed foods filled with preservatives and chemical additives as the majority over lets say veggies and whole grains non processed products, then we are telling industry as a whole that is what we want. So there will little to know changes made. Sorry for the rant but I think it was a very good video and important one to make.
We as a species need to start to realize there is no such thing as a consequence less life, and that it starts with everything we do or do not do on a daily basis right from our own homes.

Thanks again this really hit me hard.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: spot on mate and very nicely put - thanks for posting

Want to change my VW e-golf battery with one I build with the hope of a faster charge time, not really going for more energy density, but hey if I can then great, you guys are great teachers and you are going to change this world, the rest of us now just have to care to listen...

author: @aldesrochers
text: There is political battle around the cobalt...  Here in Canada, we have a cobalt mine around 110km from my home that count there reserve at 20% of the global prediction  and it is closed since the 60`s..  They were talking that Elon Musk have bought the mine and he is waiting for the cobalt to raise in price to open the mine again..  Sad situation..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is - the driver isn't cobalt - it's cheap cobalt and that is a different thing
